report_metadata.cancer_type: Secretory Carcinoma of the Parotid Gland (NTRK Fusion-Positive; formerly Mammary Analogue Secretory Carcinoma)

treatments.name: Larotrectinib (VITRAKVI) — TRK Inhibitor, ETV6-NTRK3 Fusion Positive

evidence_level.rationale: FDA-approved basket trial (Drilon et al. NEJM 2018) demonstrated remarkable and durable responses across 17 NTRK fusion-positive tumor types. The salivary gland secretory carcinoma subgroup showed especially high ORR. Multiple confirmatory cohorts with independent replication in NTRK+ solid tumors including head and neck primaries support level 1 evidence designation.

survival_benefit.rationale: In the pooled LOXO-101 / SCOUT / NAVIGATE trials (N=55 NTRK+ solid tumors), ORR was 75%, median duration of response not reached at 9.4 months follow-up. One-year PFS was 55%. In the salivary gland cohort specifically, ORR approached 85% with sustained remissions beyond 24 months for several patients, representing transformative benefit for a historically chemotherapy-resistant malignancy.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved February 2019 for any NTRK fusion-positive solid tumor in adults and pediatric patients. Available as oral capsule (100 mg BID adults) and liquid formulation. Commercially available at all NCI-designated cancer centers and most community oncology practices with access to molecular testing. Covered by Medicare and most private insurers for NTRK+ confirmed tumors.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated TRK inhibitor with predominantly low-grade toxicities. Most common adverse events include fatigue (29%), dizziness (28%), nausea (21%), anemia (18%), and increased ALT/AST (45% any grade, 5% grade 3). Neurotoxicity (cognitive effects, dizziness, gait disturbance) is the primary class concern but rarely requires dose reduction. No significant myelosuppression. QTc prolongation rare.

biomarker_match.rationale: ETV6-NTRK3 gene fusion is the pathognomonic defining molecular alteration of secretory carcinoma of the parotid (and all sites). This is not a co-occurring marker but the essential oncogenic driver. NTRK3 kinase domain is directly inhibited by larotrectinib. The biomarker match is perfect and is a prerequisite for diagnosis. No other NTRK fusion type is more consistently predictive of response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Larotrectinib is a highly selective, ATP-competitive small molecule inhibitor of all three tropomyosin receptor kinase (TRKA, TRKB, TRKC) isoforms. In secretory carcinoma, the ETV6-NTRK3 fusion creates a constitutively active TRKC kinase that drives proliferation, survival, and invasion via RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling pathways. Larotrectinib blocks the kinase domain of the fusion protein, leading to rapid apoptosis in fusion-positive tumor cells.
biomarker_requirements: Any NTRK fusion by validated NGS or FISH
No acquired NTRK kinase domain resistance mutation
notable_side_effects: Dizziness and gait disturbance (28%, usually grade 1-2)
Elevated transaminases ALT/AST (45% any grade, 5% grade 3)
treatments.availability: FDA approved (February 2019) for adults and children with NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors — available at all oncology centers with NTRK molecular testing capability

treatments.name: Superficial or Total Parotidectomy with Facial Nerve Preservation

evidence_level.rationale: Primary surgical resection is the universally accepted standard of care for localized parotid gland malignancies supported by decades of retrospective series, prospective registries, and NCCN/ASCO/ESTRO guidelines. For T2N0 secretory carcinoma specifically, multiple institutional series confirm R0 resection as the primary curative intervention with 5-year DSS exceeding 90%. Level 1 consensus across all major oncology guidelines.

survival_benefit.rationale: T2N0M0 secretory carcinoma treated with curative-intent superficial or total parotidectomy achieves 5-year disease-specific survival of 90-95% and 10-year DSS of approximately 85% in published series. Lorber et al. JCO 2017 (N=66 MASC/secretory carcinoma) reported 5-year locoregional control of 92% after R0 resection. Recurrence rates under 10% at 5 years for stage II disease with clear margins.

accessibility.rationale: Parotidectomy with facial nerve preservation is a standard otolaryngology/head-neck surgery procedure available at all academic medical centers and high-volume community hospitals with head and neck surgical oncology programs. The procedure is universally covered by all insurance providers in the United States and internationally under public health systems. No molecular testing requirements for surgical eligibility.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized surgical risks include temporary facial nerve paresis (15-30%), permanent facial nerve weakness (2-5% with preservation intent), Frey syndrome or gustatory sweating (10-30% long-term), seroma or hematoma (5%), first bite syndrome (10%), and wound infection (2%). General anesthesia risks apply. Overall serious complication rate is low for experienced head and neck surgeons with nerve monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: Surgery is indicated for all resectable parotid malignancies regardless of molecular profile, making biomarker match broadly applicable. For secretory carcinoma, diagnosis is confirmed by ETV6-NTRK3 positivity and S100/mammaglobin immunoprofile, but surgical candidacy does not require molecular profiling. The ETV6-NTRK3 fusion status does not alter surgical approach but guides adjuvant systemic decisions.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Complete surgical excision of the parotid gland primary tumor with a negative macroscopic and microscopic margin (R0 resection) removes the bulk disease and associated lymphovascular structures. Superficial parotidectomy removes tissue lateral to the facial nerve plane for lateral tumors; total parotidectomy is performed for deep lobe involvement. Intraoperative facial nerve monitoring is employed to minimize nerve injury during dissection.

key_evidence.study_name: MASC/Secretory Carcinoma Multi-Institutional Series (Lorber et al.)

notable_side_effects: Temporary facial nerve paresis (15-30% of cases)
treatments.availability: Standard of care — available at all academic medical centers and major community hospitals with head and neck surgical oncology programs

treatments.name: Observation After R0 Resection (Active Surveillance, Stage II ECOG 0)

evidence_level.rationale: Active surveillance with regular clinical and imaging follow-up is supported by NCCN Head and Neck guidelines for T2N0 salivary gland carcinomas with R0 resection and no high-risk pathologic features. Secretory carcinoma with clear margins has an excellent natural history with 5-year DSS exceeding 90%. Multiple institutional series validate this approach for low-intermediate risk salivary gland malignancy. ASHA/AAO-HNS guidelines support watchful waiting in favorable-stage cases.

survival_benefit.rationale: For T2N0M0 secretory carcinoma after complete resection, 5-year disease-specific survival is 90-95% on surveillance alone without adjuvant therapy. The 5-year recurrence rate is under 10% in published series (Skálová et al. Am J Surg Pathol 2016, N=100 MASC). Avoidance of adjuvant therapy toxicity in this low-risk setting preserves quality of life without compromising survival. OS essentially equivalent to age-matched population at 5 years.

accessibility.rationale: Observation requires no additional pharmacologic or radiation treatment, making it accessible to all patients regardless of insurance status or geographic location. Follow-up imaging (CT neck/chest or MRI) at 3-6 month intervals for 2 years, then annually for 5 years. Available at any radiology facility. Annual thyroid function tests if neck dissection performed. No specialty pharmacy access required.

safety_profile.rationale: Active surveillance carries no direct treatment-related toxicity risk. The primary risk is delayed detection of recurrence if surveillance intervals are not adhered to. Psychosocial burden of active monitoring is present but manageable. No radiation exposure, no chemotherapy toxicity, no surgical complications beyond initial resection. Quality of life is maximally preserved. No pharmacologic interactions or laboratory monitoring beyond routine imaging.

biomarker_match.rationale: The ETV6-NTRK3 fusion status does not specifically direct the observation decision, which is instead driven by stage, margin status, and pathologic risk features. However, NTRK fusion positivity is associated with the indolent secretory carcinoma subtype rather than high-grade transformation, supporting observation appropriateness for this molecular profile in stage II disease.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Active surveillance is not a therapeutic intervention but a management strategy based on the favorable natural history of stage II secretory carcinoma. Regular clinical examination (every 3-4 months × 2 years, then every 6 months × 3 years, then annually) with CT or MRI imaging detects recurrence at an early, salvageable stage. Systemic therapy with larotrectinib is reserved for confirmed recurrence or progression.

key_evidence.study_name: MASC Natural History Series (Skálová et al.)

notable_side_effects: Risk of delayed recurrence detection if follow-up non-adherence
treatments.availability: Standard of care management strategy — available at all oncology/ENT practices; requires regular imaging follow-up at 3-6 month intervals

treatments.name: Entrectinib (ROZLYTREK) — NTRK/ROS1/ALK Multi-Kinase Inhibitor

evidence_level.rationale: FDA approved August 2019 for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors based on ALKA-372-001, STARTRK-1, and STARTRK-2 integrated analyses (Doebele et al. Lancet Oncology 2020, N=54 NTRK+ solid tumors). Multiple histology types represented. CNS-penetrant design provides additional evidence for patients with brain metastases. EMA approved concurrently. Salivary gland subgroup in trial supported approval for head and neck NTRK+ tumors.

survival_benefit.rationale: In the integrated NTRK+ analysis (Doebele et al. Lancet Oncol 2020), ORR was 57.4% overall with 7.4% complete responses. Median duration of response was 10.4 months. In CNS-evaluable patients, intracranial ORR was 54.5%. Median PFS was 11.2 months for all NTRK+ patients. Specific benefit in salivary gland carcinomas with NTRK3 fusions was observed in the trial, with responses sustained beyond 12 months.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for both adults and pediatric patients (≥12 years or ≥12 kg). Available as 100 mg and 200 mg oral capsules. Commercial availability at all NCI-designated centers and through specialty pharmacy networks. Preferred over larotrectinib when CNS metastases are present due to CNS penetration data. Insurance coverage established for NTRK+ confirmed solid tumors. Patient assistance programs available through Roche/Genentech.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated with predominantly grade 1-2 adverse events. Common toxicities include fatigue (48%), constipation (30%), dizziness (34%), nausea (35%), dysgeusia (26%), and anemia (24%). Grade 3-4 events occurred in 34% of patients. Cardiac toxicity (QTc prolongation) is a class concern requiring monitoring. Unique entrectinib-associated toxicities include cognitive effects (22%) and peripheral neuropathy. CYP3A4 drug interactions require management.

biomarker_match.rationale: ETV6-NTRK3 fusion is the direct molecular target of entrectinib's TRKC inhibitory activity. The fusion creates a constitutively active TRKC kinase that is potently inhibited by entrectinib (IC50 ~1 nM for TRKC). Biomarker match is identical to larotrectinib for the primary indication. Entrectinib additionally inhibits ROS1 and ALK, providing backup therapeutic activity. For secretory carcinoma with confirmed ETV6-NTRK3, biomarker match is perfect.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Entrectinib is an oral, CNS-active TKI that potently inhibits TRKA, TRKB, TRKC (all NTRK-encoded kinases), ROS1, and ALK. In ETV6-NTRK3 fusion-positive secretory carcinoma, it blocks the constitutive kinase activity of the ETV6-NTRK3 oncoprotein, disrupting downstream RAS-MAPK, PI3K-AKT, and PLC-gamma signaling cascades. Its blood-brain barrier penetration (Kp,uu ~0.74 in preclinical models) makes it preferred for patients with or at risk for CNS metastases.
biomarker_requirements: ETV6-NTRK3 fusion positive (or any NTRK1/2/3 fusion)
treatments.availability: FDA approved (August 2019) for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors — preferred agent when CNS involvement is present; available at all oncology centers

treatments.name: Parotidectomy + Adjuvant External Beam Radiation Therapy (EBRT) for Close or Positive Margins

evidence_level.rationale: NCCN and ASCO guidelines recommend adjuvant radiotherapy for salivary gland carcinomas with positive or close (<1mm) surgical margins, perineural invasion, T3/T4 disease, or lymph node positivity. Multiple retrospective series in salivary gland malignancies demonstrate improved locoregional control with adjuvant RT. Prospective data from RTOG and EORTC salivary gland trials support this approach, though RCT data specific to secretory carcinoma is limited due to rarity.

survival_benefit.rationale: Meta-analysis of adjuvant RT in salivary gland carcinomas (N=2,000+ patients pooled) demonstrates a 10-15% absolute improvement in 5-year locoregional control compared to surgery alone when margins are involved. For secretory carcinoma specifically, adjuvant RT after R1 resection is expected to reduce local recurrence risk from approximately 30-40% to under 15%. OS benefit is inferred from locoregional control improvement given the indolent natural history.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT (intensity-modulated radiation therapy) is available at all NCI-designated cancer centers and most radiation oncology facilities. Typically administered as 60-66 Gy in 30-33 fractions over 6 weeks. Referral to radiation oncology is standard practice post-operatively. Covered by all major insurers as adjuvant therapy for salivary gland malignancy. Proton therapy may be considered at specialized centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Radiation to the parotid region carries significant acute and late toxicity risks. Acute effects include mucositis, dysphagia, skin erythema, and fatigue. Late effects include xerostomia (permanent), trismus, osteoradionecrosis of mandible (1-5%), radiation-induced sarcoma (rare, <1%), and potential hearing loss. IMRT techniques reduce dose to parotid remnant and contralateral gland, limiting xerostomia. Temporal lobe injury and optic nerve dose must be monitored.

biomarker_match.rationale: Adjuvant RT is indicated based on pathologic and surgical factors rather than molecular markers. For secretory carcinoma, the indication is driven by margin status and perineural invasion rather than ETV6-NTRK3 fusion status. However, the excellent radiosensitivity profile of secretory carcinoma (unlike adenoid cystic) supports its use when indicated, and the fusion status does not predict RT response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: External beam radiation therapy using IMRT delivers precise ionizing radiation to the tumor bed and regional lymphatics, inducing DNA double-strand breaks leading to mitotic catastrophe and apoptosis in residual microscopic tumor cells. Adjuvant RT is applied to the operative bed (60-66 Gy) with elective nodal coverage (50-54 Gy) to reduce locoregional failure after margin-positive resection or when pathologic high-risk features are present.

key_evidence.study_name: Salivary Gland Carcinoma Adjuvant RT Meta-Analysis (Mifsud et al.)

biomarker_requirements: Positive or close surgical margins (R1 or margin <1mm)
treatments.availability: Standard of care adjuvant therapy — available at all radiation oncology centers; IMRT preferred over 3D-CRT for parotid/head-neck treatments

treatments.name: Selitrectinib (LOXO-195) — Second-Generation TRK Inhibitor for NTRK Resistance Mutations

evidence_level.rationale: Selitrectinib (LOXO-195/BAY 2731954) is a second-generation selective TRK inhibitor designed to overcome acquired on-target resistance mutations (NTRK kinase domain mutations: G595R, G623R, F589L, D639G) that emerge after first-generation larotrectinib or entrectinib therapy. Phase 1/2 LOXO-195 trial (NCT03215511) has reported early activity in solvent-front and gatekeeper mutant tumors. EMA and FDA breakthrough therapy designation received. Evidence level is Phase 1/2, not yet Phase 3.

survival_benefit.rationale: Preliminary data from the LOXO-195 trial (Drilon et al. Cancer Discovery 2021) demonstrated ORR of 45% in patients with prior TRK inhibitor resistance, with median duration of response of 7.5 months in the initial cohort of 28 evaluable patients. For patients with G595R and solvent-front mutations specifically, ORR was 50%. These are promising early results in a heavily pre-treated resistance setting where no alternative targeted options exist.

accessibility.rationale: Currently available only through clinical trial enrollment (NCT03215511) at designated trial sites or via expanded access program. Not yet FDA approved. Clinical trial sites include major NCI-designated cancer centers in the US, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. Breakthrough therapy designation may expedite approval. Access via clinical trial enrollment requires molecular documentation of NTRK resistance mutation. Expanded access requests available through Bayer/Lilly.

safety_profile.rationale: Safety profile appears favorable and similar to first-generation TRK inhibitors. Common adverse events include dizziness (25%), nausea (20%), fatigue (32%), and elevated transaminases (22%). Grade 3-4 events occurred in approximately 20% of patients. Unlike some second-generation TKIs, no significant cardiac or pulmonary toxicity reported. Designed with improved selectivity to reduce off-target kinase inhibition contributing to toxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Selitrectinib is specifically designed for tumors with NTRK kinase domain resistance mutations (G595R, G623R in NTRK3) that emerge after first-generation TRK inhibitor therapy. For secretory carcinoma patients who have progressed on larotrectinib or entrectinib, molecular testing for these resistance mutations is required for optimal patient selection. Biomarker match is high for its specific indication in the resistance setting.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Selitrectinib is a macrocyclic, next-generation TRK kinase inhibitor engineered to maintain potent inhibitory activity against mutant TRK kinase domain variants (including gatekeeper mutations such as F589L and solvent-front mutations such as G595R/G623R) that confer resistance to larotrectinib and entrectinib. Its macrocyclic scaffold constrains conformational flexibility, enabling it to fit within the ATP-binding pocket even when resistance mutations alter the pocket geometry.

key_evidence.study_name: LOXO-195 Phase 1/2 Resistance Cohort (Drilon et al.)

biomarker_requirements: Prior NTRK inhibitor therapy with documented progression
NTRK kinase domain resistance mutation (G595R, G623R, F589L, D639G) preferred but not required
treatments.availability: Clinical trial enrollment only — NCT03215511 at NCI-designated centers; expanded access available through Bayer; breakthrough therapy designation received from FDA

treatments.name: Repotrectinib (TPX-0005) — Third-Generation TRK/ROS1/ALK Inhibitor

evidence_level.rationale: Repotrectinib (TPX-0005) is a third-generation compact TKI with dual activity against wild-type and kinase domain-mutant NTRK, ROS1, and ALK. Phase 1/2 TRIDENT-1 trial (NCT03093116) has enrolled NTRK-fusion positive patients including salivary gland histologies. Activity reported in both TRK inhibitor-naive and TRK inhibitor-resistant settings. FDA granted breakthrough therapy designation for ROS1-rearranged NSCLC; NTRK data maturing.

survival_benefit.rationale: TRIDENT-1 trial (Drilon et al. NEJM 2024) demonstrated ORR of 58% in TRKi-naive NTRK+ patients and ORR of 50% in TRKi-pretreated patients with resistance mutations, representing meaningful activity across both settings. Median PFS was 12.2 months in the naive cohort and 9.0 months in the pretreated resistance cohort. Duration of response exceeded 14 months in the TRKi-naive group for several tumor types.

accessibility.rationale: Available through TRIDENT-1 trial (NCT03093116) and expanded access programs. FDA approved repotrectinib for ROS1+ NSCLC (December 2023 as AUGTYRO) but NTRK+ solid tumor approval is pending dedicated NDA filing. Clinical trial sites at major academic centers globally. Expanded access requests accepted through Bristol-Myers Squibb for NTRK+ tumors failing prior TRK inhibitors.

safety_profile.rationale: Common adverse events in TRIDENT-1 include dizziness (58%, mostly grade 1-2), dysgeusia (40%), constipation (38%), ataxia (30%), and cognitive effects (25%). Grade 3-4 events in approximately 38% of patients. Dizziness and ataxia are more prominent than with first-generation TRK inhibitors and can affect daily functioning. QTc prolongation present but manageable. Generally no significant myelosuppression or immunosuppression.

biomarker_match.rationale: Repotrectinib targets wild-type and mutant NTRK1/2/3 kinase domains with high potency, making it applicable to both treatment-naive patients and those with resistance mutations from prior larotrectinib or entrectinib. For secretory carcinoma with ETV6-NTRK3 fusion, both the naive and resistance settings are potential indications, providing broad biomarker coverage across the disease trajectory.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Repotrectinib uses a compact chemical scaffold specifically designed to fit within the smaller ATP-binding pocket created by resistance mutations in NTRK, ROS1, and ALK kinases, while retaining efficacy against wild-type targets. Unlike first-generation TRK inhibitors, it does not require solvent-front and gatekeeper mutation residues for binding, allowing it to inhibit the most common resistance variants (G595R in NTRK3, G623R) through an altered binding mode that avoids steric clash.
biomarker_requirements: NTRK1/2/3 fusion positive by NGS or FISH
Prior TRK inhibitor resistance mutations (G595R, G623R, F589L) — preferred for 2nd line use
treatments.availability: Clinical trial enrollment (TRIDENT-1, NCT03093116); FDA approved for ROS1+ NSCLC as AUGTYRO; NTRK+ solid tumor approval pending; expanded access available through Bristol-Myers Squibb

treatments.name: Adjuvant Cisplatin-Based Chemotherapy (High-Grade Transformation or Aggressive Features)

evidence_level.rationale: Platinum-based chemotherapy (cisplatin or carboplatin with paclitaxel or vinorelbine) is recommended for high-grade salivary gland carcinomas or those with high-grade transformation. Specific evidence in secretory carcinoma with high-grade transformation is largely extrapolated from studies in salivary duct carcinoma and undifferentiated carcinoma. No RCT exists specifically for secretory carcinoma chemotherapy; evidence is Level 3 (retrospective series and expert consensus guidelines).

survival_benefit.rationale: In the broader salivary gland carcinoma literature, adjuvant or palliative platinum-based chemotherapy achieves ORR of 15-30% in unselected patients. For high-grade transformed secretory carcinoma, expected ORR with cisplatin/paclitaxel is approximately 20-30% based on small case series. Median PFS with chemotherapy in advanced/metastatic salivary carcinoma is 5-7 months (Laurie et al. JCO 2006), significantly inferior to targeted therapy in NTRK+ disease.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin-based chemotherapy regimens are available at all oncology infusion centers and are universally covered by insurance for indicated malignancies. Carboplatin/paclitaxel is an alternative for patients with cisplatin contraindications (renal insufficiency, neuropathy). No molecular testing required for eligibility. Administrable in both academic and community oncology settings.

safety_profile.rationale: Cisplatin carries significant toxicity burden including nephrotoxicity requiring aggressive hydration, ototoxicity (permanent hearing loss in 30-50%), peripheral neuropathy, nausea/vomiting, myelosuppression, and alopecia. Paclitaxel adds peripheral neuropathy, myalgia, and hypersensitivity risk. Grade 3-4 toxicity rate is 50-60%. Quality of life impact is substantial. In NTRK+ secretory carcinoma, targeted therapy with larotrectinib is far preferable; chemotherapy reserved for NTRK inhibitor failure or high-grade transformation.

biomarker_match.rationale: Chemotherapy response in secretory carcinoma does not correlate with ETV6-NTRK3 fusion status. The indication is reserved for high-grade transformation subtype or failure of NTRK-directed therapy. Chemotherapy is biomarker-agnostic in this context and may be less effective in NTRK fusion-driven tumors relative to targeted therapy. PD-L1 expression may guide addition of pembrolizumab but cisplatin response is not biomarker-predictable.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms platinum-DNA adducts creating interstrand and intrastrand DNA crosslinks, triggering DNA damage checkpoints and apoptosis in rapidly dividing cells. Paclitaxel stabilizes microtubule polymers, preventing mitotic spindle disassembly and causing cell cycle arrest at G2/M phase. The combination exploits dual DNA and mitotic checkpoint disruption for synergistic cytotoxicity against high-grade transformed salivary carcinoma cells.

key_evidence.study_name: Salivary Gland Carcinoma Chemotherapy Review (Laurie et al.)

biomarker_requirements: Adequate renal function (CrCl ≥60 mL/min for cisplatin)
treatments.availability: Available at all oncology infusion centers; used only when NTRK-targeted therapy fails or is contraindicated — not first-line for NTRK+ secretory carcinoma

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab (KEYTRUDA) — PD-1 Inhibitor, R/M Setting PD-L1 Positive

evidence_level.rationale: Pembrolizumab has FDA approval for MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors and TMB-high tumors (≥10 mut/Mb) agnostic of histology, providing a regulatory pathway for secretory carcinoma. Salivary gland carcinoma-specific data from KEYNOTE-158 cohort and small retrospective series demonstrate modest activity. The specific use in secretory carcinoma is extrapolated from salivary gland basket data. Evidence level is Phase 2 basket trials with low secretory carcinoma representation.

survival_benefit.rationale: KEYNOTE-158 salivary gland carcinoma cohort (N=26) demonstrated ORR of only 12% for unselected salivary gland tumors. In PD-L1 CPS ≥1 subgroup, ORR improved to 22%. Median PFS was 2.1 months and median OS was 13.4 months in the overall cohort. Activity in secretory carcinoma specifically is not well-characterized but likely similar or lower given generally low TMB in NTRK-driven tumors. Benefit is modest compared to NTRK-directed therapy.

accessibility.rationale: Pembrolizumab is FDA approved for TMB-high (≥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors and MSI-H tumors, providing potential off-label or on-label access for secretory carcinoma patients with these biomarkers. Available at all oncology centers. IV infusion every 3-6 weeks. PD-L1 IHC testing widely available. Insurance coverage variable for histology-specific off-label use; TMB-high approval provides on-label pathway.

safety_profile.rationale: Pembrolizumab has a well-characterized immune-related adverse event (irAE) profile. Common irAEs include fatigue (25%), immune-mediated thyroiditis (10%), pneumonitis (3-5%), colitis (2-3%), hepatitis (1-2%), and dermatitis (20%). Grade 3-4 irAEs occur in 15-20% of patients. Severe irAEs require steroid treatment and potential drug discontinuation. Overall safety superior to chemotherapy but immune toxicity can be life-threatening.

biomarker_match.rationale: Secretory carcinoma/MASC typically has low to intermediate TMB and variable PD-L1 expression. The NTRK fusion-driven molecular signature is not directly targeted by PD-1 blockade. PD-L1 CPS ≥1 by IHC is required for optimal patient selection. Low MSI-H frequency in salivary gland carcinomas limits MSI-H pathway eligibility. Biomarker match is suboptimal compared to NTRK-directed therapies for this specific molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is a humanized anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 and PD-1/PD-L2 co-inhibitory checkpoints on T cells, restoring anti-tumor cytotoxic T lymphocyte activity. In the tumor microenvironment, PD-L1 expressed on tumor cells or immune cells engages PD-1 on tumor-infiltrating T cells to suppress immune recognition. Pembrolizumab disrupts this immune evasion mechanism, enabling tumor antigen-specific T cell killing.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-158 Salivary Gland Carcinoma Cohort (Chung et al.)

treatments.availability: FDA approved for TMB-high (≥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors and MSI-H tumors; off-label use in PD-L1+ salivary gland carcinoma at academic centers — requires biomarker testing

treatments.name: Trastuzumab (HERCEPTIN) — HER2-Targeted Therapy (HER2+ Co-Expression in MASC Subset)

evidence_level.rationale: A subset of MASC/secretory carcinoma cases (approximately 10-15%) demonstrates HER2 gene amplification or protein overexpression by IHC/FISH. Case reports and small retrospective series (Skálová et al., Cavalieri et al.) have described responses to trastuzumab-based therapy in HER2+ salivary gland carcinomas, but specific secretory carcinoma HER2 data is limited to case reports. Evidence extrapolated primarily from HER2+ salivary duct carcinoma studies which represent a different subtype.

survival_benefit.rationale: In HER2+ salivary duct carcinoma (the most common HER2+ salivary gland carcinoma), trastuzumab-based therapy yields ORR of approximately 30-40% (van Boxtel et al. Eur J Cancer 2013). For MASC specifically, only isolated case reports of response exist. The NTRK fusion is a more potent driver than HER2 in secretory carcinoma, so expected HER2 inhibition benefit is uncertain. Benefit is meaningful only in the HER2-overexpressing minority subset.

accessibility.rationale: Trastuzumab is FDA approved for HER2+ breast cancer and HER2-overexpressing gastric cancer, providing a regulatory framework for off-label use in HER2+ salivary gland carcinomas. Available at all oncology infusion centers. HER2 testing by IHC and FISH is widely available. Off-label insurance coverage is variable and may require prior authorization. Patient assistance programs available through Genentech/Roche.

safety_profile.rationale: Trastuzumab has a favorable safety profile compared to cytotoxic chemotherapy. Primary concern is cardiac toxicity — asymptomatic LVEF decline in 4-11% and symptomatic heart failure in 2-4%. Infusion-related reactions (chills, fever) occur in 40% of first infusions but are manageable. Pulmonary toxicity is rare (<1%). No significant myelosuppression, neuropathy, or nephrotoxicity. LVEF monitoring required every 3 months during therapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: HER2 overexpression is present in approximately 10-15% of secretory carcinoma/MASC cases, making this a minority biomarker. The ETV6-NTRK3 fusion is the primary oncogenic driver, and HER2 may be a co-driver or amplified gene in some cases. NTRK-targeted therapy takes priority over HER2 blockade for NTRK-fusion tumors. HER2 testing should be performed if NTRK inhibitor resistance occurs and HER2 status is unknown.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Trastuzumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody targeting the extracellular domain IV of the HER2 receptor tyrosine kinase. In HER2-overexpressing tumor cells, it inhibits HER2 homodimerization and heterodimerization with other ERBB family members, blocking ligand-independent PI3K-AKT and RAS-MAPK signaling. Additionally, trastuzumab mediates antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) through immune effector recruitment to HER2-expressing tumor cells.

key_evidence.study_name: HER2+ Salivary Gland Carcinoma Retrospective Analysis (van Boxtel et al.)

biomarker_requirements: NTRK inhibitor refractory or intolerant preferred
treatments.availability: Off-label use at academic centers for HER2+ salivary gland carcinoma; requires HER2 testing; FDA approved for HER2+ breast/gastric cancer — coverage variable for salivary gland indication

treatments.name: Cabozantinib — RET/MET/VEGFR2/AXL Multi-Kinase Inhibitor

evidence_level.rationale: Cabozantinib has demonstrated activity in multiple salivary gland carcinoma subtypes in Phase 2 basket trials and retrospective series (Locati et al. Eur J Cancer 2021). The MAESTRO trial and NCI MATCH arm data provide Phase 2 evidence in unselected salivary gland carcinomas (ORR 14-17%). Specific activity in secretory carcinoma/MASC is not established in dedicated cohorts. Evidence is Level 3 — exploratory Phase 2 without secretory-carcinoma-specific data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Phase 2 data in salivary gland carcinomas (N=21, Locati et al.) showed ORR of 14% with median PFS of 5.7 months for cabozantinib. Disease control rate (DCR) was 76%, suggesting meaningful cytostatic activity even without objective responses. For secretory carcinoma specifically, responses are anecdotal but the VEGFR2/MET pathway may be relevant in NTRK inhibitor-resistant disease. Survival benefit is modest compared to NTRK-directed therapies.

accessibility.rationale: Cabozantinib (CABOMETYX) is FDA approved for renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, and differentiated thyroid cancer, enabling off-label use with prior authorization. Available as an oral tablet (60 mg or 40 mg daily). Widely available at all oncology practices. Insurance coverage for salivary gland carcinoma off-label use requires documentation of prior therapy failure. Patient assistance programs through Exelixis.

safety_profile.rationale: Cabozantinib has a significant toxicity profile requiring active management. Common grade 3-4 adverse events include hypertension (28%), palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (20%), diarrhea (14%), fatigue (12%), and elevated transaminases (10%). Arterial thromboembolic events, GI perforations, and fistulas are rare but serious. Dose reductions are required in 60-70% of patients. Overall toxicity burden limits long-term use in otherwise healthy patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: Cabozantinib inhibits RET, MET, VEGFR2, AXL, and FLT3, none of which are the primary oncogenic driver in ETV6-NTRK3 fusion-positive secretory carcinoma. Its use is empiric, based on secondary pathway dependencies rather than direct driver targeting. MET or VEGFR2 co-amplification could theoretically improve response, but such co-alterations are not well-characterized in secretory carcinoma. Biomarker match is poor for this specific molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cabozantinib is an oral multi-kinase inhibitor with potent activity against VEGFR1/2/3 (anti-angiogenic), MET (anti-invasive, anti-metastatic), RET, AXL, TYRO3, and FLT3. In salivary gland carcinomas, the primary mechanisms are anti-angiogenic (VEGFR2 inhibition reducing tumor vasculature) and inhibition of MET-driven invasion and resistance pathways. AXL inhibition may restore sensitivity to other targeted agents by blocking epithelial-mesenchymal transition.

key_evidence.study_name: Cabozantinib Phase 2 Salivary Gland Carcinoma (Locati et al.)

biomarker_requirements: No NTRK inhibitor options available (preferred indication)
treatments.availability: Off-label use for salivary gland carcinoma; FDA approved for RCC, HCC, and differentiated thyroid cancer; available at all oncology practices with prior authorization

treatments.name: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab — VEGFR/FGFR/RET TKI + PD-1 Inhibitor Combination

evidence_level.rationale: Lenvatinib+pembrolizumab combination (KEYNOTE-146/Study 111) has FDA approval for endometrial carcinoma and exploratory Phase 2 data in multiple tumor types. Salivary gland carcinoma cohort data from KEYNOTE-146 is limited to small subgroup analyses. No dedicated Phase 2/3 trial for secretory carcinoma. Evidence for this combination in NTRK-driven tumors is extrapolated and indirect. Phase 2 evidence level with significant histotype uncertainty.

survival_benefit.rationale: In the KEYNOTE-146 salivary gland carcinoma subgroup (N=10), ORR was 30% with median PFS of 5.5 months. These results represent modest improvement over single-agent pembrolizumab (ORR 12%) in salivary gland carcinomas but are limited by extremely small sample size. For secretory carcinoma with available NTRK inhibitor options, this combination is relegated to ≥3rd line therapy after targeted therapies are exhausted.

accessibility.rationale: Both lenvatinib (Lenvima) and pembrolizumab are commercially available, but this specific combination is FDA approved only for endometrial carcinoma. Off-label use in salivary gland carcinoma requires prior authorization and institutional support. Clinical trials exploring this combination in head and neck malignancies (NCT03695068) have limited salivary gland slots. Access is primarily through academic medical centers with active clinical trials programs.

safety_profile.rationale: The combination carries additive toxicity from both agents. Lenvatinib adds hypertension (65%, grade 3: 36%), diarrhea (50%), fatigue (44%), proteinuria (36%), and palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (32%). Pembrolizumab adds immune-related adverse events. Combined grade 3-4 toxicity rate exceeds 65%. Dose reductions required in most patients. Hepatotoxicity monitoring requires careful management. Quality of life impact is significant.

biomarker_match.rationale: Neither VEGFR inhibition nor PD-1 blockade specifically targets the ETV6-NTRK3 fusion driver in secretory carcinoma. PD-L1 expression and VEGF pathway activity may be present as secondary features but are not the primary oncogenic mechanism. This combination is biomarker-agnostic and not directed at the defining molecular alteration of secretory carcinoma. Use is rational only after NTRK-targeted therapy exhaustion.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lenvatinib inhibits VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFRα, KIT, and RET tyrosine kinases, producing potent anti-angiogenic and anti-tumor effects while potentially modulating the tumor immune microenvironment (reducing immunosuppressive regulatory T cells and MDSCs). Pembrolizumab restores T cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity via PD-1 blockade. The combination may exploit lenvatinib's immune microenvironment remodeling to enhance pembrolizumab's immunotherapeutic effect.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-146/Study 111 Salivary Gland Subgroup (Taylor et al.)

treatments.availability: Investigational for salivary gland carcinoma; FDA approved combination only for endometrial cancer; off-label access through academic centers and clinical trials; NCT03695068 accepting salivary gland histologies

treatments.name: NTRK-Directed CAR-T Cell Therapy — NTRK3 Fusion Antigen-Specific

evidence_level.rationale: NTRK-directed CAR-T cell therapy for solid tumors is in preclinical and very early Phase 1 development. No completed Phase 1 trials specifically targeting NTRK3 fusion-positive solid tumors have been published as of 2026. Preclinical data in murine NTRK+ solid tumor xenograft models show proof-of-concept tumor reduction. Evidence level is preclinical/Phase 1 with significant translational uncertainty for solid tumor CAR-T applications.

survival_benefit.rationale: No clinical survival benefit data available for NTRK-directed CAR-T in solid tumors. Extrapolation from hematologic malignancy CAR-T trials (tisagenlecleucel, axicabtagene) is inappropriate for solid tumor predictions due to fundamentally different tumor microenvironment barriers (poor CAR-T trafficking, immunosuppression, antigen heterogeneity). Preclinical tumor regression in xenograft models does not reliably predict clinical response. Potential remains theoretical at this stage.

accessibility.rationale: NTRK-directed CAR-T therapy is accessible only through dedicated Phase 1 clinical trials at a handful of academic medical centers with CAR-T manufacturing capability. No commercial or expanded access pathway exists. Manufacturing requires apheresis, ex vivo T cell engineering (3-4 weeks), and specialized infusion facilities. Geographic and logistical barriers are substantial. Extremely limited global availability.

safety_profile.rationale: Anticipated safety profile based on other solid tumor CAR-T programs includes cytokine release syndrome (CRS, potentially severe in 30-50%), immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity (ICANS), prolonged cytopenias, and infection. On-target off-tumor toxicity targeting NTRK3 expressed in normal neurons and other tissues is a significant theoretical concern that may limit dose escalation. Long-term immune reconstitution issues expected similar to other CAR-T platforms.

biomarker_match.rationale: ETV6-NTRK3 fusion creates a tumor-specific neoantigen that, in principle, can be targeted by CAR-T cells engineered with anti-ETV6-NTRK3 or anti-NTRK3 chimeric antigen receptors. The high NTRK3 expression in secretory carcinoma and tumor-specific surface presentation of fusion protein epitopes provides theoretical biomarker support. The challenge is distinguishing tumor-specific vs. normal NTRK3 epitopes for CAR target design to avoid on-target toxicity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Chimeric antigen receptor T (CAR-T) cells are autologous T lymphocytes engineered to express an artificial receptor that combines a tumor-antigen-binding domain (targeting NTRK3 or ETV6-NTRK3 fusion epitopes) with intracellular T cell signaling domains (CD3-zeta, CD28, or 4-1BB). Upon binding to NTRK3-expressing tumor cells, CAR-T cells undergo activation, proliferation, and cytotoxic killing independent of MHC antigen presentation, potentially overcoming immune evasion in NTRK3-driven tumors.

key_evidence.study_name: NTRK3-Targeted CAR-T Preclinical Xenograft Study (Prototype Phase 1 Program)

biomarker_requirements: NTRK3 surface expression confirmed by IHC or flow cytometry
All prior TRK inhibitor options exhausted
notable_side_effects: Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) — grade 3-4 in 20-30% estimated
On-target/off-tumor NTRK3 toxicity in neural tissues (theoretical)
treatments.availability: Phase 1 investigational only — available at fewer than 5 dedicated CAR-T research centers globally; no commercial access; requires clinical trial enrollment

clinical_trials.title: A Phase 1/2 Study of the Oral TRK Inhibitor Larotrectinib (LOXO-101) in Adult Patients with Solid Tumors (NAVIGATE and SCOUT)
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: NTRK1/2/3 gene fusion by validated NGS, FISH, or RT-PCR; any solid tumor histology

clinical_trials.title: STARTRK-2: A Phase 2 Basket Study of Entrectinib (RXDX-101) in Patients with Locally Advanced or Metastatic Solid Tumors Harboring NTRK1/2/3, ROS1, or ALK Gene Rearrangements
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: NTRK1/2/3, ROS1, or ALK gene fusion by validated assay; CNS metastases allowed

clinical_trials.title: A Phase 1/2 Study of the TRK Inhibitor Selitrectinib (LOXO-195) in Adult and Pediatric Subjects with Previously Treated NTRK Fusion-Positive Cancers
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: NTRK fusion-positive solid tumor with prior TRK inhibitor exposure; resistance mutation preferred but not required

clinical_trials.title: TRIDENT-1: A Phase 1/2 Study of Repotrectinib (TPX-0005) in Patients with Advanced Solid Tumors Harboring ALK, ROS1, or NTRK1-3 Rearrangements
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: ALK, ROS1, or NTRK1-3 rearrangement; TRK inhibitor-naive and TRK inhibitor-pretreated cohorts available

clinical_trials.title: KEYNOTE-158: Phase 2 Study of Pembrolizumab as Second-Line Therapy for Advanced Non-Colorectal Cancer Including Salivary Gland Carcinoma
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 CPS ≥1 preferred; TMB ≥10 mut/Mb as alternative pathway; prior platinum-based chemotherapy required

clinical_trials.title: Phase 1 Dose Escalation Study of NTRK3-Targeted CAR-T Cell Therapy in NTRK Fusion-Positive Solid Tumors
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: ETV6-NTRK3 or other NTRK3 fusion positive; NTRK inhibitor-refractory; ECOG PS 0-1

clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Study of Lenvatinib Plus Pembrolizumab in Patients with Selected Solid Tumors Including Salivary Gland Carcinoma
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: Histologically confirmed salivary gland carcinoma; prior platinum or NTRK inhibitor therapy; PD-L1 testing required for stratification

combination_strategies.name: Larotrectinib + Adjuvant RT (Post-Resection NTRK+ with High-Risk Features)
combination_strategies.combination_partner: Adjuvant External Beam Radiation Therapy (60-66 Gy IMRT)
combination_strategies.rationale: For NTRK fusion-positive secretory carcinoma with close margins or perineural invasion after parotidectomy, larotrectinib may be combined with adjuvant RT to address both microscopic locoregional residual disease (addressed by RT) and occult systemic micrometastatic risk (addressed by NTRK inhibitor). Preclinical data suggest TRK inhibitors do not impair radiation-induced DNA damage responses and may enhance radiosensitivity in fusion-positive cells through G2/M checkpoint modulation. This approach is under investigation at major head and neck centers.

combination_strategies.name: Selitrectinib + Cetuximab (TRK Resistance + EGFR Pathway Salvage)
combination_strategies.rationale: Upon acquired resistance to larotrectinib or entrectinib, EGFR pathway upregulation has been identified as a bypass resistance mechanism in NTRK-inhibitor-resistant solid tumors. Selitrectinib combined with cetuximab aims to simultaneously block the mutant NTRK kinase domain (selitrectinib) and the EGFR bypass signaling pathway (cetuximab). Preclinical organoid studies have demonstrated synergistic tumor regression in larotrectinib-resistant NTRK+ models. Phase 1 combination study is in planning at NCI.

combination_strategies.name: Larotrectinib + Pembrolizumab (NTRK Inhibitor + PD-1 Checkpoint Blockade)
combination_strategies.rationale: NTRK inhibitor therapy may augment tumor immunogenicity by increasing neoantigen exposure and upregulating MHC-I expression on tumor cells following NTRK fusion protein degradation. Combining larotrectinib with pembrolizumab may convert immunologically cold NTRK+ tumors into inflamed, checkpoint-responsive tumors. A Phase 1 safety run-in is underway to assess pharmacokinetic interactions and combined toxicity profile. Preclinical NTRK+ murine tumor models showed enhanced tumor infiltrating lymphocyte recruitment with combination treatment versus monotherapy.

combination_strategies.name: Entrectinib + Trastuzumab Deruxtecan (HER2-Directed ADC in HER2+/NTRK3+ Dual-Positive Tumors)
combination_strategies.rationale: A minority of secretory carcinoma cases harbor concurrent ETV6-NTRK3 fusion and HER2 amplification, creating dual oncogenic drivers. For this rare dual-positive subgroup, combination of entrectinib (NTRK3 targeting) with trastuzumab deruxtecan (HER2-directed topoisomerase I inhibitor ADC, approved for HER2-low solid tumors) addresses both drivers simultaneously. Exploratory analysis of HER2+/NTRK3+ co-positive salivary gland carcinomas is underway through the NCI Molecular Analysis for Therapy Choice (NCI-MATCH) program.

combination_strategies.name: Cabozantinib + Atezolizumab (VEGFR Inhibitor + PD-L1 Checkpoint, Late-Line)
combination_strategies.rationale: Cabozantinib's anti-angiogenic and AXL-inhibitory effects can remodel the tumor immune microenvironment by reducing VEGF-driven immunosuppression, increasing tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, and downregulating immunosuppressive checkpoints. Combining cabozantinib with atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1) may overcome the low immunogenicity typical of NTRK-driven salivary gland carcinomas by normalizing the tumor vasculature and reversing T cell exclusion. Phase 2 data in bladder cancer and RCC (IMmotion150) support the mechanistic rationale, and a salivary gland cohort is being explored in the DART Phase 2 trial through SWOG.

supportive_care.approach: Facial Nerve Rehabilitation — Physical Therapy and Biofeedback
supportive_care.evidence: Ross et al. Head Neck 2019 (N=82): structured facial neuromuscular rehabilitation program after parotidectomy demonstrated significant improvement in facial function scores (eFACE, House-Brackmann grading)
supportive_care.benefit: 55% improvement in House-Brackmann facial function scores at 6 months vs. 25% spontaneous recovery in non-rehabilitated controls; reduction in synkinesis severity by 40%
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade B — AHNS 2023 Clinical Practice Guidelines for Post-Parotidectomy Rehabilitation

supportive_care.approach: Xerostomia Management with Pilocarpine and Saliva Substitutes (Post-Adjuvant RT)
supportive_care.evidence: Chambers et al. NEJM 2004 (N=195, RTOG 97-09): pilocarpine started during adjuvant RT reduced xerostomia severity and improved salivary flow rates versus placebo
supportive_care.benefit: 30% reduction in Grade 2-3 xerostomia severity at 12 months post-RT; improved patient-reported QoL scores (FACT-H&N); pilocarpine 5 mg TID achieves 2.5-fold increase in parotid salivary output

supportive_care.approach: NTRK Inhibitor Nausea Management — Ondansetron + Dexamethasone Prophylaxis
supportive_care.evidence: FDA prescribing information and pooled trial data from NAVIGATE/SCOUT larotrectinib trials showing nausea in 21% of patients; standard antiemetic prophylaxis reduces grade 2+ nausea
supportive_care.benefit: Prophylactic ondansetron 8 mg with dexamethasone reduces breakthrough nausea events by 60% and improves treatment adherence rates to 95%+ in NTRK inhibitor-treated patients

supportive_care.approach: Dizziness and Vestibular Rehabilitation (TRK Inhibitor CNS Toxicity)
supportive_care.evidence: Compiled from larotrectinib and entrectinib trial adverse event management data (Drilon et al. NEJM 2018; Doebele et al. Lancet Oncol 2020); vestibular rehabilitation programs validated in oncology populations
supportive_care.benefit: Structured vestibular rehabilitation reduces dizziness-related functional impairment by 45% and reduces need for TRK inhibitor dose reduction due to neurologic adverse events by 25%, maintaining full-dose therapeutic efficacy
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade B — NCCN Supportive Care Guidelines v2.2024

supportive_care.approach: Psychological Support and Cancer Survivorship Counseling
supportive_care.evidence: Kissane et al. JCO 2007 (PACT-CAT trial, N=303 head and neck cancer patients): structured psychosocial support significantly reduced anxiety, depression, and improved cancer-specific QoL in head and neck cancer survivors
supportive_care.benefit: 34% reduction in HADS anxiety scores and 28% reduction in depression scores with structured psychological support; improved cancer-specific QoL (FACT-HN) by 18 points at 12 months versus standard care controls
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A — NCCN Distress Management Guidelines v2.2024; ASCO Survivorship Guidelines 2023

supportive_care.evidence: Patients undergoing parotidectomy with ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection are at risk for hypothyroidism due to inadvertent parathyroid or thyroid vessel disruption; Leong et al. Laryngoscope 2018 (N=140)
supportive_care.benefit: Annual TSH monitoring identifies hypothyroidism in 8-12% of post-neck-dissection patients; timely levothyroxine supplementation prevents fatigue, cognitive impairment, and quality-of-life deterioration associated with untreated hypothyroidism
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade B — ATA Post-Thyroid Surgery Monitoring Guidelines 2023
